question_text: Hi IT, we have a weird phone issue: incoming calls from some customers never reach us anymore. Callers say it either gives a busy tone or drops after a few seconds, but other customers can still call us normally. It seems to be only one specific set of numbers (it started after we got a new number block from the provider last week). In Teams we don’t even see the incoming call attempt. Can you check what this is, and please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Summary - Short answer: This matches a known Direct‑Routing failure where a newly provisioned carrier number block was not present in the SBC translation/normalization rules. The carrier confirmed delivery to your SIP trunk/SBC but the SBC answered INVITEs for the new range with 404 (so Teams sees nothing). A temporary SBC translation rule restored calls in tests. Based on this, classify the issue as 2nd‑level (Unified Communications / SBC) work, not a pure 1st‑level helpdesk ticket. This conclusion is based on the incident record and remediation pattern in your tickets [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74, 76)]. What the records say (evidence) - The ticket records the carrier's statement that calls for the affected numbers were delivered to your SIP trunk / SBC - i.e., delivery to your edge was confirmed by the provider [Data: Sources (8)]. - SBC logs in the incident show INVITEs for the affected (new) range being answered with "404 Not Found" while calls from other numbers were forwarded to Teams. Applying a temporary normalization/translation rule for the new range restored delivery in tests and a permanent change was later applied [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74, 154)]. - Teams Admin Center showed no incoming call entries for the failing calls (absence of a Teams event is consistent with the SBC answering locally and not forwarding the call) [Data: Sources (8); Reports (76)]. - Related incidents in the dataset also show different root causes (media/RTP or NAT session timeouts) that produce short connects or ~30s drops, so if you see a brief connect rather than immediate busy/404 you should also investigate NAT/session timers and firewall RTP port rules [Data: Sources (16); Reports (23)]. Likely root cause(s) - Primary: Missing/incorrect SBC translation/normalization entry for the newly provisioned number block -> SBC answers INVITE with 404 and does not forward to Teams. Temporary translation rule validated this in the recorded incident [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74)]. - Secondary (if symptom differs): carrier routing/porting propagation issues or site network (NAT/firewall) issues (e.g., short UDP/NAT timeout causing BYE at ~29-31s or firewall blocking dynamic RTP ports causing one‑way audio). These are separate failure modes in the records and should be checked in parallel if media symptoms appear [Data: Sources (16); Reports (23)]. Immediate 1st‑level triage steps you can run now (collect these before escalating) 1. Gather the affected sample numbers (e.g., the ticket used +41 44 785 23 10 and +41 44 785 23 11) and approximate timestamps of failed calls - capture any user screenshots of busy tones [Data: Reports (74); Sources (8)]. 2. Check Teams Admin Center for the incoming-call telemetry for those sample times and numbers (confirm absence of an incoming call event) [Data: Sources (8); Reports (76)]. 3. Ask the carrier to confirm delivery and (if possible) to provide a SIP trace or CDR for the sample calls (INVITE timestamps, Call‑ID, and the carrier trunk IP/port they used). The carrier confirmation narrows the fault domain to your SBC if they show delivery to your trunk [Data: Sources (8)]. 4. If you have SBC access, search SBC logs for INVITEs for the sample numbers and note the SIP response (404 vs 200). If you see immediate 404 answers, capture those log snippets for escalation [Data: Sources (8)]. Why this should be escalated to 2nd‑level (recommended classification) - The likely fix requires editing SBC translation/normalization (dial‑plan) rules and coordinating with the carrier for test calls and traces - tasks that require SBC/Direct Routing privileges and UC expertise, not just front‑line steps. The incident record shows this exact work was performed by UC/SBC specialists and resolved the outage after adding the new range mappings [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74, 76)]. - If media problems or ~30s drops are observed after calls succeed, network/NAT/firewall adjustments are also required (Network + UC teams) [Data: Sources (16); Reports (23)]. Recommended 2nd‑level actions (what the UC/SBC team should do) 1. Export SBC translation/normalization table and confirm the new carrier block is present and normalized to E.164 as expected. If missing, add a targeted entry for the block (apply as a temporary rule first to validate). The prior incident used a temporary translation rule to validate the fix before making it permanent [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74)]. 2. Coordinate a short, time‑boxed test window with the carrier: ask them to place test calls from sample numbers while you capture SBC SIP traces and packet captures on the SBC external interface. Request carrier SIP traces/pcaps for the same calls to correlate delivery (Call‑ID and timestamps) [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74)]. 3. Correlate carrier trace -> SBC trace -> Teams Admin Center events (produce a simple correlation table: Call‑ID, SBC timestamp UTC, SBC event (INVITE/404/200/BYE), Teams timestamp/event). If SBC saw INVITE->404 while carrier shows delivery to trunk, you have a translation/routing failure to fix [Data: Sources (8); Reports (76)]. 4. If calls succeed but drop or have media issues, check SBC Session‑Expires and site Voice‑VLAN NAT/UDP session timeout (a 30s timeout has produced BYE at ~29-31s in the records) and verify firewall allows the dynamic RTP ranges in use (don't only allow 3478-3481) [Data: Sources (16); Reports (23)]. 5. After changes, run carrier‑originated tests, capture SBC logs/pcaps, and confirm Teams Admin Center shows incoming calls and successful media. Suggested wording to request from the carrier (copy/paste) - Request full SIP traces (INVITE->final response) and per‑message timestamps (epoch_ms or ISO‑8601 with timezone), CDR lines for the sample calls, and the carrier source IP/port used for trunk delivery. This is exactly what was requested in the incident workflow to correlate delivery [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74)]. I can draft that email for you if you want. Offer / next steps I can take for you - I recommend treating this as a 2nd‑level (UC/SBC + Network) ticket. If you want, I can: (a) draft the carrier request email and the SBC log extraction checklist, (b) produce the correlation table template (CSV headers) to map carrier -> SBC -> Teams events, and (c) prepare the short maintenance/test script to apply a temporary translation rule and validate. The incident record includes these same steps and templates used successfully [Data: Sources (8); Reports (74, 76)]. If you'd like me to draft the carrier email and the exact SBC‑log/pcap commands (or a short test script), tell me the sample numbers you want tested and whether you have SBC access or the SBC vendor/model - I'll produce copy‑pasteable artifacts for the UC/SBC and carrier teams.

question_text: Hi Helpdesk, during Teams calls I can hear everyone perfectly, but nobody can hear me. It happens on internal and external calls. Teams shows my USB headset mic selected, but the little mic/input bar never moves at all. Even the Teams test call records nothing. Chat works fine. I already unplugged and replugged the headset but it didn’t change. Please help, and confirm: is this a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Short summary - Your symptom (you can hear others, others can't hear you; Teams shows the USB headset selected but the mic/input bar never moves and Test Call records nothing) most commonly indicates a local hardware or OS-level capture problem - often fixed by first-line (helpdesk) actions. In one documented case the headset was visible to Teams and Windows but the mic cable inside the headset was not fully inserted; reseating that cable immediately restored the Windows input meter and the Teams Test Call succeeded [Data: Sources (37); Reports (44)]. Immediate steps to run now (do these in order) 1. Check the Windows input meter first: Settings -> System -> Sound -> Input (or Control Panel -> Sound -> Recording). Speak and watch whether any input bar moves. 2. If the Windows meter is flat: fully unplug the headset, reseat any detachable mic/boom connector on the headset (if present), and plug the headset directly into a PC USB port (bypass hubs/docks). Try a different physical USB port (rear/host port preferred). Reseating the mic cable fixed the cited case [Data: Sources (37); Reports (44)]. 3. If still flat, test the same headset on another PC (and/or try a known-good headset on this PC) to isolate headset vs. PC. The results of these swaps determine next steps. 4. If the Windows meter moves but Teams still records nothing: verify Windows Microphone privacy (Settings -> Privacy & security -> Microphone) and ensure Teams is allowed to use the mic, then restart Teams and re-run a Test Call. OS privacy settings have caused similar device-access issues in our records [Data: Sources (42)]. 5. If you reach driver/port ambiguity: check Device Manager -> Audio inputs and outputs / Sound controllers for errors and note any warning icons (capture screenshots for escalation). Is this a 1st‑level or 2nd‑level ticket? - Start as a 1st‑level (helpdesk) ticket. The typical L1 runbook is hardware-first: reseat mic connector, port swap, test on another PC, check Windows input meter, verify privacy permissions, run Teams Test Call - many cases (including the one referenced) were resolved by first-line via a hardware reseat and Test Call validation [Data: Sources (37); Reports (44)]. - Escalate to 2nd‑level (UC/network or endpoint engineering) if ANY of the following apply: the Windows input meter remains flat after trying multiple ports and another known-good headset; Device Manager shows driver errors you can't resolve; multiple users at the same site have identical symptoms (site/network/media path issue); or the OS shows input but Teams still won't transmit after permissions and client checks. Site‑wide or Direct‑Routing/SBC/firewall media issues required 2nd‑level/UC and network work in other incidents [Data: Sources (23); Sources (5); Sources (16)]. What to send/helpdesk evidence to speed resolution - Short answers to: Windows input meter moved? (Yes/No). Have you tried another USB port, the PC's direct port vs dock, and another PC/headset? - Attach screenshots: Teams -> Settings -> Devices (showing mic selection and input bar) and Windows Sound -> Input (showing device and meter). If you escalate, also include Device Manager screenshot(s) for "Audio inputs and outputs" and any warning icons. In similar tickets those screenshots + a Test Call result sped escalation or closure [Data: Sources (37)]. If you want, I will - Provide a one‑page L1 checklist you can paste to your helpdesk or the user, and/or draft the exact escalation text (including which screenshots/logs to attach) if you confirm the results of the steps above. References (examples from prior incidents) - Single‑user USB headset mic was selected in Teams and Windows but showed no input until the headset mic cable was reseated; reseat fixed Test Call immediately [Data: Sources (37); Reports (44)]. - OS app privacy blocking devices has caused camera/mic access problems that were fixed by enabling microphone access for apps/Teams [Data: Sources (42)]. - Multi-user / site‑wide one‑way audio or session drop issues were escalated to UC/network teams (firewall/SBC/NAT/session timers) in other incidents [Data: Sources (23); Sources (5); Sources (16)]. Tell me the Windows input‑meter result (yes/no), whether you're plugged via dock/hub or directly, and whether you've tried another PC or headset - I'll tell you the exact next action and, if needed, produce the escalation text.

question_text: Hi Helpdesk, one user’s camera basically disappeared in Teams. In Teams settings there is no camera option at all. We also tried Teams in the browser and it still can’t use the camera. The weird part is: Windows Device Manager still shows the camera device, so it doesn’t look physically broken. The user says Windows updated recently. Can you help and confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Short answer - start as 1st‑level (helpdesk) triage, escalate to 2nd‑level only if one of the escalation triggers below is met. Rationale - When Device Manager shows the camera but Teams (desktop) and Teams in the browser cannot use it, the pattern most commonly points to an OS‑level permission/configuration issue (Windows privacy settings or app permissions) or a driver/service regression triggered by an update. In the documented case the camera was visible in Device Manager but app access was blocked by Windows privacy settings and the helpdesk restored camera access by enabling the OS/app permissions; that ticket was handled and closed at first line [Data: Sources (42); Reports (5)]. - The user‑reported recent Windows Update makes an update/drivers hypothesis plausible, but the specific camera ticket does not record a KB ID; checking the device's update history is part of triage rather than proof of causation [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19)]. 1st‑level (what to try and collect before escalating) Do these checks and capture the artifacts below - these are routine helpdesk actions and in the referenced incident they restored camera access without escalation [Data: Sources (42)]: Quick tests (try in this order) 1. Open Windows Camera app - does it show a live image? (If yes, hardware/driver are likely OK; focus on Teams/browser permissions.) 2. Settings -> Privacy & security -> Camera - ensure "Camera access for this device", "Let apps access your camera" and "Let desktop apps access your camera" are ON and Teams/browser are allowed. After changing, restart Teams and re‑test. (This exact fix resolved the documented ticket) [Data: Sources (42)]. 3. Test Teams in an Incognito/private browser window (to exclude cached tokens/extensions) and check browser site camera permissions. 4. In Teams desktop: sign out, clear cache (or Repair app), restart, and test again. 5. Device Manager: try Disable -> Enable the camera; inspect Properties -> Driver for Provider / Driver Version / Driver Date. Capture those values if present. If Device Manager shows a warning or error code, collect it (that pushes toward escalation). 6. Temporarily close virtual‑camera apps and check AV/endpoint webcam‑protection settings (do not leave AV disabled longer than necessary). If disabling webcam protection restores access, capture AV logs/settings. Useful commands / quick collections to paste into the ticket - Get driver metadata (PowerShell): Get-CimInstance Win32_PnPSignedDriver | Where-Object { $_.DeviceName -match "(?i)camera" } | Select DeviceName, Manufacturer, DriverVersion, DriverDate - Get most recent installed update (PowerShell): Get-HotFix | Sort-Object InstalledOn -Descending | Select-Object -First 3 - Collect Teams logs (user): press Ctrl+Alt+Shift+1 while reproducing the issue (creates a logs ZIP in Downloads). (These collection steps are part of the recommended evidence set used in the runbook) [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19)]. Escalation (when to treat as 2nd‑level) Escalate to 2nd/endpoint engineering when any of the following apply - these are the documented escalation triggers in the runbooks and incidents: - The Windows privacy setting is greyed out, reverts after change, or gpresult/rsop shows a policy disabling camera access (i.e., GPO/Intune enforcement). - Device Manager reports driver errors (Code 10, driver failing to start) or driver reinstall/rollback is required and needs admin/vendor driver packages. - Evidence the recent Windows update or a pushed driver caused a regression and requires rollback or vendor escalation. - Multiple users or many devices in the same OU/MDM group show identical symptoms (suggests a deployment/policy issue). - Event Viewer shows CameraFrameServer/driver/kernel errors that need deeper debugging. When escalating, include the artifacts below - they speed the 2nd‑level investigation [Data: Reports (5); Reports (131)]. Artifacts to attach to the escalation ticket - Device Manager screenshot plus a screenshot of the Driver tab (Provider, Version, Date). - Windows Settings -> Privacy -> Camera screenshot showing the toggles and allowed apps. - Teams desktop Devices pane screenshot (showing "no camera" or missing options). - Teams logs.zip (Ctrl+Alt+Shift+1) and the exact reproduction timestamps (timezone). - Event Viewer exports (.evtx) for System and the CameraFrameServer/DeviceSetupManager logs covering the reproduce window. - Output of Get-HotFix (or a screenshot of Settings -> Update history) showing recent KBs. - Short note whether the device is Intune/SCCM enrolled or domain‑joined (dsregcmd /status, presence of Company Portal, or SMS/CcmExec). (These are the items the documented runbook & tickets recommend collecting) [Data: Sources (42); Reports (5)]. Notes & practical advice - Start as 1st‑level: the referenced incident was resolved by enabling OS camera/app permissions at helpdesk level, which is a standard first‑line activity and should be attempted first [Data: Sources (42)]. - Do not assume the Windows Update is the cause until you can correlate an installed KB/driver date with the symptom - the camera ticket did not include a KB, so collection of Get-HotFix / Update History is important before deciding rollback [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19)]. - If AV/webcam‑shield or virtual camera apps are present, include their logs/settings in your triage; the camera ticket did not show AV as the cause, but AV/virtual cams are common interveners and should be checked before escalation [Data: Sources (42)]. If you want, I'll produce: - A one‑page, copy‑pasteable 1st‑level checklist you can give the technician, and - A short escalation template (exact filenames, commands and a list of artifacts to attach) you can paste into your 2nd‑level handover. References (examples from the incident corpus) - The specific camera case was resolved by enabling Windows app camera permissions and handled at helpdesk (Employee 33) [Data: Sources (42)]. - The runbook/triage guidance and escalation triggers described above match the Teams Client Devices & Permissions blueprint and related incident summaries [Data: Reports (5); Reports (131)]. - Where a Windows Update was mentioned separately in the corpus, staff were advised to collect the KB via Get‑HotFix or Update History to correlate with endpoint failures [Data: Sources (19); Sources (42)]. If you want the 1st‑level checklist and escalation template now, say which ticketing fields your team uses (owner, severity, attachments) and I'll format them for copy/paste.

question_text:
Hi IT, sorry for the long msg but Finance is blocked and I’m trying to give all details.

In D365 F&O, when we go General Ledger -> Financial Reports -> Edit, it either errors sometimes or it just sits there loading forever. It’s not just me: multiple EMEA colleagues report the same. What’s throwing everyone off: older reports now show the creator as 'unknown' (like the system forgot who made them), and when we try to create/open a report we also got a 'report cannot be found' type message.

We tried the usual noise: different browser, cleared cache, rebooted, even tried from a different laptop. Same result. And this is happening in UAT AND PROD so we can’t even test in the other env to workaround. Also unrelated but maybe not: yesterday someone said they applied a 'security baseline' on some servers.

Can you tell what category this fits and what to check first, and please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Below is a concise diagnosis, priority checklist and next steps you can use immediately. Short summary first, then an ordered list of checks and what to collect for escalation. Summary / quick diagnosis - This looks like a platform/infrastructure problem (AOS/IIS/TLS + Financial Reporting Data Mart / FRDB / service‑account identity resolution) that is preventing the Financial Reports / Report Designer stack from reading report metadata (creators showing as "unknown") and from opening/creating reports. Multiple EMEA users and both UAT and PROD are affected, so treat this as a cross‑environment, business‑impacting incident [Data: Reports (55); Sources (0)]. - The incident history shows a prior successful remediation pattern: applying a best‑practice TLS/cipher profile (IISCRYPTO) and restarting AOS/IIS returned service - which strongly points at TLS/cipher negotiation or Schannel differences as a prime suspect, but the dataset does not prove a prior baseline push caused the outage (the recorded cipher change is the corrective action, not a proven pre‑incident change) [Data: Reports (55); Sources (25)]. - The AOS nodes 001‑SRV‑AOS72 (reported "fast" node) and 001‑SRV‑AOS73 (sluggish) were specifically called out during triage and should be compared first for configuration drift [Data: Reports (53); Sources (1)]. - FRDB / Data Mart work was performed in the integration environment (D365FO_INTE) during the investigation (FRDB recreation and Data Mart Reset were recorded) - this confirms DB/Data Mart checks were part of triage, but the final fix in the ticket was the TLS/cipher + AOS restart combo [Data: Reports (53); Sources (1); Reports (55)]. Support classification - Classify as a 2nd‑level (platform/infrastructure) incident. Rationale: multi‑user, multi‑environment impact; root‑cause touches AOS/IIS/TLS, identity lookups and the FRDB backend; previous work required infra changes and vendor escalation. This is beyond desktop/browser 1st‑line troubleshooting [Data: Reports (55); Sources (0); Sources (25)]. Immediate (priority) checklist - do these in order 1. Capture scope/data (high priority) - Collect screenshots, example report names/IDs, affected usernames, exact timestamps (UTC if possible), and which environment(s). These artifacts are essential for log correlation and vendor escalation [Data: Reports (53); Sources (0)]. 2. Compare TLS / cipher suite and Schannel settings across IIS and each AOS node (especially 001‑SRV‑AOS72 vs 001‑SRV‑AOS73) - Configuration drift here frequently produces node‑specific behavior (one node works, others fail). If you have IISCrypto, export the profile on the IIS host; on each AOS run Get‑TlsCipherSuite and check the registry SSL Functions key. If you find mismatches, align them and then restart AOS/IIS (see step 4) [Data: Reports (55); Reports (141); Sources (25)]. 3. Verify service‑account / identity resolution and AAD sync - "Creators shown as 'unknown'" commonly indicates the reporting service cannot resolve author identities (service account token failures, AAD/AD lookup problems). Validate the service account credentials/permissions and check Azure AD/Azure AD Connect sync or token errors for the service account and affected users [Data: Sources (25); Reports (56)]. 4. Restart order (if you change TLS/cipher settings) - If you apply or revert cipher/TLS changes, perform them synchronously across IIS and all AOS nodes and then restart AOS and IIS per runbook. AOS restart is required for Schannel/cipher changes to fully take effect; the incident history shows this sequence resolved a prior outage [Data: Reports (55); Sources (25)]. 5. Validate FRDB / Data Mart and SQL connectivity - Confirm FRDB is ONLINE, that report metadata tables contain expected rows (sample the metadata rows), and that the Financial Reporting service can connect as the service account. The team did recreate FRDB in integration and ran Data Mart resets during triage, so DB-level state must be checked and correlated with service/auth logs [Data: Reports (53); Sources (1)]. 6. If smoke tests still fail, escalate to Microsoft with a reproducible package - Gather the artifacts below and open vendor case with exact repro steps, timestamps and the collected logs [Data: Reports (55)]. Short actionable steps you can run now (low risk) - Recycle the Financial Reporting IIS app‑pool(s) or do an IIS reset on one node (preferably in a maintenance window) and retest. If that fixes the symptom temporarily, it indicates a worker‑process / cached credential or TLS session issue; if not, proceed to the TLS/FRDB checks above. Note: because this spans PROD and UAT and affects business users, coordinate the action with ops and test on one node first [Data: Reports (53); Sources (1)]. - On the two AOS nodes (001‑SRV‑AOS72 and 001‑SRV‑AOS73) run a direct test: RDP to each server and launch Report Designer locally. Compare behavior (this node‑vs‑node test was used in triage previously) [Data: Reports (53); Sources (1)]. Logs and artifacts to collect (essential for root‑cause) - From each AOS node and IIS host (time window around a failing attempt + a buffer): - Windows System and Application event logs (include Schannel events). - Get-TlsCipherSuite output and registry export of the SSL Functions key (or IISCrypto export). - IIS W3C logs + Failed Request Tracing (FREB) for the reporting site; HTTPERR if present. - AOS service/application traces and the Financial Reporting service logs. - SQL Server ERRORLOG for the FRDB and SQL Agent job history (to spot connectivity/login failures or restores). - Optional: short network capture (netsh trace) during a repro to show TLS ClientHello / ServerHello and cipher negotiation. These artifacts are the ones the incident analysis says to collect to prove TLS handshakes, node drift and FRDB access errors [Data: Reports (55); Reports (53); Sources (25)]. Example quick collection commands (run as Administrator) - use only if you can - Export cipher suites: - Get-TlsCipherSuite | Sort Name > C:\temp\CipherSuites_$(hostname).txt - Export Schannel events (last 200): - Get-WinEvent -LogName System -FilterXPath "*[System[Provider[@Name='Schannel']]]" -MaxEvents 200 | Format-List TimeCreated, Id, Message > C:\temp\Schannel_$(hostname).txt - IIS logs: copy C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\W3SVC* files for the time range. (If you want the full script used previously by the incident team to collect across all nodes, say so and I'll produce it.) [Data: Reports (141); Reports (55); Sources (25)] What the records do NOT confirm (so don't assume yet) - I cannot confirm from the provided records that a "security baseline" change was the explicit root cause - the tickets record that PROD had modifications over a weekend and that a baseline was mentioned by someone, but the corpus does not include a pre‑incident change log proving a baseline push immediately preceded the failure. The documented cipher change was recorded as the corrective action during remediation, not as the proven trigger in the artifacts you shared [Data: Sources (0); Reports (55)]. Who to involve / escalation path - Immediately involve 2nd‑level platform/ops (IIS, AOS, Windows infra), the DBA(s) owning FRDB/SQL, and the identity team (AAD / Azure AD Connect) for service‑account checks and token/claim analysis. Open an escalation to Microsoft if you can reproduce after collecting the artifact bundle (the prior incident used Microsoft support during the same outage) [Data: Reports (55); Sources (1)]. If you want me to help now - I can: (A) provide a small PowerShell script to collect cipher lists, event exports and Schannel snippets from the three key hosts (IIS + AOS72 + AOS73) for you to run and paste the outputs here; or (B) if you paste the three exported files (cipher lists + Schannel + a short IIS log sample) I will diff the TLS/cipher settings and point to the exact mismatch and recommend precise suites to enable/disable and the restart order [Data: Reports (55); Reports (141); Sources (25)]. Bottom line - Treat as 2nd‑level / platform incident. First focus: compare TLS/cipher settings across IIS and AOS nodes (001‑SRV‑AOS72 vs 001‑SRV‑AOS73), verify service‑account identity resolution and FRDB connectivity, collect Schannel/IIS/AOS/SQL logs and then perform coordinated cipher alignment + AOS/IIS restarts per the Management Reporting runbook. The incident history shows that sequence fixed a prior occurrence, so it's the highest‑probability remediation path to test after you gather the logs [Data: Reports (55); Reports (53); Sources (25)]. If you want the ready‑to‑run collection script or want me to review logs you collect, say which hosts/timestamps you can share and I'll produce the next artifact set and an annotated plan for the maintenance window.

question_text:
Hi IT, external customers can’t log in to our customer portal since this week and it’s getting escalated.

From the office network, login works fine. From outside (home internet / customer side) the browser shows '502 Bad Gateway'. We already checked the basics on our side: DNS looks fine, cert looks valid, HTTPS is reachable. Someone said a new certificate was installed over the weekend and there was also a firewall/security update.

The weird part: if we bypass the normal address and hit the reverse proxy directly (different way of reaching it), it behaves differently, but via the normal portal URL it fails.

I know this sounds like 'clear cookies', but we tested multiple browsers and devices and it’s the same. What is the likely cause, and please confirm: should this be classified as 1st-level or 2nd-level support?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: ### Short answer Most likely this is a perimeter (WAF / reverse‑proxy) problem: a recent security/patch activity caused the WAF to reload a default inspection/profile and the Portal‑App‑Template (with the custom header‑rewrite that forwards X‑Forwarded‑Proto) was not active - as a result the backend did not see X‑Forwarded‑Proto and returned 502s for external FQDN requests. This is a 2nd‑level (Network & Security / WAF) support issue and should be escalated accordingly. [Data: Sources (15); Reports (13)] --- ### Why that fits your symptoms - External clients see "502 Bad Gateway" while office (internal) users work - the incident record shows that when the publishing/WAF layer strips or fails to forward X‑Forwarded‑Proto during TLS inspection, the backend rejects or misinterprets the request even though the backend itself and LB health checks remain healthy [Data: Sources (15); Reports (13)]. - Direct‑to‑reverse‑proxy (bypass) worked while FQDN failed - consistent with name‑based/profile selection at the perimeter (a different template or SNI/virtual‑host mapping applied for the FQDN path) or with header rewrite being present on the direct path but absent on the public/published path [Data: Sources (15)]. - A certificate was installed over the weekend and a firewall/security patch ran at the same time; the ticket shows the cert was validated and not the root cause, while the patch triggered a profile/template reload that removed the Portal‑App‑Template (the header rewrite) - tying the outage to the perimeter change rather than DNS or the app itself [Data: Sources (15); Sources (17)]. --- ### Immediate actions to take (priority order) 1. Escalate to Network & Security / WAF owners (2nd‑level) immediately. The remediation requires editing/appplying WAF templates or inspection policy changes [Data: Sources (15)]. 2. Verify active template/profile for the portal virtual server and confirm the custom header‑rewrite that injects X‑Forwarded‑Proto: https is present. If it isn't, re‑apply the Portal‑App‑Template from your canonical blueprint and test. [Data: Sources (15); Sources (17)] 3. Reproduce externally and capture headers (to prove the failure): from an external host run a verbose curl and force the portal FQDN to the reverse‑proxy IP (example): - curl -v --resolve portal.kf40.ch:443:<REVERSE_PROXY_IP> https://portal.kf40.ch/ Capture the verbose output and response headers and provide them to the WAF team. (This exact --resolve test is a recommended diagnostic to compare FQDN vs direct‑IP behavior.) [Data: Reports (13); Reports (159)] 4. Check WAF logs and audit/change history for a template/profile reload or any config commit during the weekend patch window - look for activations of Default‑Strict or removal of Portal‑App‑Template. Correlate those timestamps to the first observed 502s. [Data: Sources (15); Reports (13)] 5. As a short, controlled test (only if authorized): temporarily disable TLS inspection for the portal object and retest external login. The ticket shows disabling inspection restored logins and re‑enabling reproduced 502 - this is useful to validate the hypothesis quickly before changing templates permanently. [Data: Sources (15)] 6. After fix, run and retain external end‑to‑end synthetic checks that assert both 200 login success and that X‑Forwarded‑Proto is present at the backend. Add this to your post‑patch validation checklist. [Data: Sources (17); Reports (13)] --- ### What to collect and attach when you escalate - The external curl verbose outputs (FQDN and --resolve tests) with timestamps. - WAF transaction/transaction‑audit lines for the failing request(s) showing request headers and the template/profile in effect. Export the request/egress pair (client->WAF and WAF->backend) if possible. If you can paste a redacted sample I will read it. [Data: Sources (15); Reports (13)] - WAF config change / audit log entries for the weekend patch window (who/what/when committed the profile reload). [Data: Sources (15)] --- ### Classification: 1st‑level vs 2nd‑level This should be handled as 2nd‑level (Network & Security / WAF) work. Initial triage steps (DNS, cert, basic reachability) are 1st‑level, but the root cause is perimeter template/profile and TLS‑inspection behavior and requires WAF operator privileges to reapply templates and validate header rewrites. The documented incident followed exactly this escalation path. [Data: Sources (15)] --- ### Extra notes / short mitigations - Don't roll back certs as first step - helpdesk validated the certificate (CN/SAN/chain) and ticket evidence shows the header/template change was the proximate cause [Data: Sources (15)]. - If you need a quick runbook, exact curl commands, or the precise WAF log fields/queries to extract (or vendor‑specific commands to reapply Portal‑App‑Template), tell me the WAF vendor and I'll draft them for you. If you want, I can also draft the short message/payload to paste into your change/alert ticket to request the WAF transaction export and the config diff for the weekend patch.

question_text:
Hi IT, Compliance is going crazy because a SharePoint folder behaves completely random and we can’t explain it.

Two weeks ago the folder was renamed (used to be something like 'Compliance_Audit', now it's 'Compliance_Review_2025'). After that they put one of those Microsoft sensitivity labels on it ('Confidential - Internal' banner at the top). Since then: some people can open it, some get access denied, and one person swears it works only if he opens the link in a private/incognito window. Same link, same user, different outcome depending on how/when he opens it.

Helpdesk already checked: the label says encryption is enabled and it mentions access is only for a specific group. But SharePoint permissions look like they are still managed by a different group. That’s what confuses everyone.

What is the most likely root cause and what should we fix, and please confirm: 1st-level or 2nd-level ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Summary - short - Most likely root cause: a mismatch between the sensitivity label's encryption ACL (the label is granting decryption to a different AAD group) and the SharePoint folder ACL (which is granting access via a different group/object). Token / SSO caching makes this appear intermittent (older cached tokens sometimes include the "old" group claim and work; refreshed tokens lack the required label group and are denied). This exact failure pattern is the primary finding in the incident records you supplied [Data: Sources (30); Reports (8)]. Why this explains "same link, same user, different outcome" - Sensitivity labels that enforce encryption require the user's token to carry the group/claim the label allows (decrypt rights). If the label references Compliance_Core but SharePoint ACLs reference SP_Compliance_Main / COMPLIANCE_TEAM (different AAD objects), a user can be shown as permitted by SharePoint yet be unable to decrypt the files controlled by the label. Token caching (old vs new token claims) produces the incognito vs normal‑session difference you observed [Data: Sources (30); Sources (31)]. - A related, common root is an Azure AD Connect / same‑displayName collision (cloud group vs on‑prem group with different objectIDs) which can silently replace the cloud group that included guests - that also fits the external‑guest/ACL divergence pattern recorded in your tenant incidents [Data: Sources (13); Reports (64)]. Immediate (short‑term) actions (can be done by Helpdesk / 1st‑level) 1. Force a full sign‑out and token refresh for affected users: sign out of all Office desktop apps and the browser (or ask users to test in Incognito). If that restores access, it strongly indicates a stale‑token/claim issue [Data: Sources (38); Sources (30)]. 2. If you need a quick workaround, add an affected user temporarily to the AAD group the label grants (Compliance_Core) - investigators saw that adding a user to the label group immediately restored access for that user [Data: Sources (30)]. 3. Collect and attach these artifacts before escalating: one affected username, exact timestamps of a failing and a succeeding attempt, a screenshot of the SharePoint Manage Access view for the folder, and a screenshot/export of the Purview label encryption panel (which group the label allows). These are the minimal items 2nd‑level will need [Data: Sources (30); Reports (31)]. Root fix (requires 2nd‑level / Identity + Compliance + SharePoint) - Align the objects used by label and ACL: - Option A (preferred for minimal disruption): reconfigure the sensitivity label (or the label policy) so the label's allowed/decrypt group is the exact AAD group object that the SharePoint ACL uses (match by ObjectID, not display name). Or reconfigure the SharePoint ACL to use the group object the label expects. Do not rely on display names - compare GUIDs [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13)]. - Option B: if the label was applied after unique permissions and "Preserve custom permissions" was not enabled, reapply the label with Preserve custom permissions (or reapply after aligning groups) so encryption maps to the folder's existing ACL mapping [Data: Sources (31); Reports (63)]. - If you find duplicate groups (same displayName, different objectId) due to Azure AD Connect, consolidate the groups and run a full sync so the authoritative object contains intended members (including guests) - this is the documented remediation used in similar incidents [Data: Sources (13); Reports (64)]. - After making identity/group/label changes, force token revocation (or advise users to sign out) so clients receive fresh tokens with the corrected group claims [Data: Sources (31); Reports (8)]. Validation steps (what 2nd‑level should do) - Reproduce with a disposable test user: add to the label group and verify access, then remove and verify denial - the incident team used this to prove label enforcement [Data: Sources (30)]. - Pull Azure AD sign‑in logs for sample users and compare token claims before/after refresh to show COMPLIANCE_TEAM vs Compliance_Core claim presence; correlate sign‑in timestamps with the folder access attempts [Data: Sources (31); Sources (38)]. - Export SharePoint permission principals (drive item permissions via Graph) and Graph / Azure AD queries of groups named COMPLIANCE_TEAM / Compliance_Core to compare objectIds and onPremisesSyncEnabled flags (detect cloud vs synced objects) [Data: Sources (13); Reports (8)]. Who should own the ticket? - Start as a 1st‑level ticket for triage: Helpdesk should run the sign‑out/incognito tests, collect screenshots, and try the temporary add‑to‑label‑group test if permitted. If evidence points to a label ↔ ACL mismatch, duplicate group objectIDs, or changes to label configuration are required, escalate to 2nd‑level (Identity / BU Infrastructure - M365 / Purview/Compliance and SharePoint engineering). The incidents in your records followed exactly this escalation path [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13)]. What to hand to 2nd‑level when you escalate (minimum evidence) - Folder path and exact timestamp of when label was applied (Purview audit), SharePoint Manage Access screenshot, Purview label encryption screenshot (which AAD group is allowed), Azure AD sign‑in entries (failing and succeeding) for at least one user (raw JSON or exported entries), and a sample user who reproduced the incognito vs normal behaviour. That evidence lets identity engineers check token claims, objectIDs, and Azure AD Connect history quickly [Data: Sources (30); Reports (8, 13)]. If you want, I can also: - Provide exact Microsoft Graph / PowerShell commands to export the folder's permissions, list groups named COMPLIANCE_TEAM/Compliance_Core and their objectIds, and query sign‑in logs for group‑claim differences; or - Draft a short runbook for Helpdesk to collect the artifacts safely (screenshots, sign‑in timestamps) before escalation. References (incident evidence summarized) - The label vs ACL mismatch with token caching as the cause is documented in your incident records [Data: Sources (30); Reports (8)]. - Token refresh / forced sign‑out as an effective first‑line remediation is recorded in helpdesk testing notes [Data: Sources (38); Sources (30)]. - The cloud vs on‑prem group collision and remediation (consolidate groups + full Azure AD Connect sync) is documented in the related ticket work [Data: Sources (13); Reports (64)]. If you want those PowerShell/Graph snippets or a one‑page runbook for Helpdesk to execute now, tell me whether you have Global Admin / Compliance Admin rights available (or whether you want only the read‑only queries) and I'll produce copy‑paste commands.

question_text:
Hi IT, we have a production issue: deliveries are blocked because SAP can’t print any delivery notes/pick lists.

This is the confusing part: normal printing from Office/follow-me works, but printing that comes out of SAP is dead. SAP jobs are sent to the usual Windows print servers (we have two dedicated ones for SAP output). We already restarted the print spooler and even rebooted one server, but it didn’t change. The only thing that happened recently: Windows updates were installed and we restored the two print-server VMs to a previous snapshot after that maintenance window.

Users are now asking to roll back Windows, but before we do something drastic: what is the most likely cause in this pattern and what should be checked/fixed first? Also please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Summary - short - Most likely cause: a Windows update + VM snapshot/restore sequence broke the SAP -> Windows print‑queue exposure (printer network‑share publishing or queue registration) or created a driver/OS mismatch on the two SAP print‑server VMs. The outage pattern you describe (Office/follow‑me printing OK; SAP prints stuck) plus the recorded restore after maintenance fits that root cause [Data: Sources (28, 29); Reports (16, 102)]. - Important note: blue‑screens (BSODs) were observed on the restored VMs in the incident timeline, so treat this as an OS/driver/hypervisor issue that needs forensic capture before broad changes [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. Most likely failure modes (ranked) 1. Printer network‑share / AD publishing or SMB exposure was corrupted by the restore (SAP jobs can't see the published queues though Office printing paths still work). Toggling the printer share is a known quick remediation in this scenario [Data: Reports (102, 28)]. 2. A driver or Windows update regression (print driver, kernel/SMB, or integration component) introduced an incompatibility; restore/rollback created a mismatch between guest drivers and the host or new OS modules and the queue‑publishing components [Data: Sources (28); Reports (16, 173)]. 3. Snapshot/restore inconsistency (registry/queue metadata, SMB bindings, or guest‑agent state not re‑published after restore), or hypervisor/host/VM‑tools mismatch revealed by the snapshot restore [Data: Sources (29); Reports (100, 173)]. What to check and do first (do these in order) 1. Stop - do not rollback production Windows yet. Preserve evidence and snapshots. The incident guidance is explicit: collect logs/dumps and preserve the snapshot used for restore before any rollback [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. 2. Confirm SAP‑to‑Windows handoff: - In SAP: check the spool requests (SP01) and note the SAP Print Target names and timestamps. This determines whether jobs leave SAP. - On the Windows printserver(s): check if those same jobs arrive in the Windows queue (Get-PrintJob / Print Management). If jobs appear on the Windows queue but never print, the fault is post‑handoff (printserver/share/driver) [Data: Sources (7); Reports (28)]. 3. Verify printer share publication and network exposure (quick, low‑risk): - Run Get-Printer and Get-SmbShare (or net share) on VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 and confirm the expected queues are Shared and Published. If the share is missing or not published, a controlled disable->enable of the printer network share often clears stuck SAP jobs (this was the remediation recorded in the outage) - but capture logs first [Data: Reports (102, 28)]. 4. Collect immediate artifacts (forensics) before further changes: - Export System and PrintService (Microsoft‑Windows‑PrintService/Operational) event logs for the restore/BSOD window. - Copy C:\Windows\Minidump\*.dmp and MEMORY.DMP if present. - Export Get-HotFix, driver list (Get-PrinterDriver or driverquery / pnputil), and Get-Printer output. These artifacts are required because BSODs were observed after the restore and are essential to safe remediation [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16, 173)]. 5. Compare drivers/versions: - Export Get-PrinterDriver (Name, Version, InfName) from the two affected SAP printservers and from a known‑good follow‑me printserver and diff them. Look for driver version changes or unsigned drivers that could explain the regression [Data: Sources (28); Reports (173)]. 6. Check Windows Update history and recent patches: - Get‑HotFix / DISM package list to identify which KBs were applied prior to the snapshot used and correlate them with the failure window. Do not assume a particular KB - confirm from the server/patch system first [Data: Sources (28); Reports (173)]. 7. If BSODs present: preserve VM snapshot and export VM memory (hypervisor level) if the VM will not produce a reliable guest dump. Analyse minidumps with WinDbg (or hand to platform team) to identify faulting module/driver. This is required because kernel crashes point at drivers or host/guest compatibility issues [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. Concrete quick checks / commands to run (examples) - On Windows printservers (run elevated): - Get-Printer | Format-Table Name,Shared,ShareName,Published - Get-SmbShare | Format-Table Name,Path,ScopeName - Get-PrintJob -PrinterName "<queue>" - Get-PrinterDriver | Select Name,Manufacturer,DriverVersion,InfName | Export-Csv C:\Temp\printerDrivers.csv - Get-HotFix | Sort InstalledOn | Export-Csv C:\Temp\hotfixes.csv - wevtutil epl System C:\Temp\System.evtx - wevtutil epl "Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational" C:\Temp\PrintService.evtx (If you want, I can provide a one‑click PowerShell collection script to export these.) These are the same categories of artifacts recommended by the incident runbook [Data: Reports (16, 100)]. Short remediation path (safe / recommended) - If the Windows queue shows SAP jobs but they are stuck: capture logs, then try a controlled disable->enable of the printer share for one queue (document timestamps and collect logs before/after). The recorded outage showed this action cleared the backlog quickly [Data: Reports (102, 28)]. - If driver/version mismatch is identified: reproduce on a clone and test driver rollback (or install the vendor-recommended driver) on the test clone first - do not perform blind production rollbacks [Data: Reports (173, 16)]. - If a specific Windows KB is implicated by hotfix timestamps and dump analysis, do a staged rollback on a clone and validate end‑to‑end SAP test prints before any production rollback. Preserve snapshots and evidence always [Data: Sources (28); Reports (16, 173)]. Ticket classification and ownership - Classify this as a 2nd‑level (infrastructure/platform) ticket - the failure surface is OS/driver/VM/hypervisor and requires server/virtualization expertise and forensic analysis (spooler restarts and reboots already did not resolve the issue) [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16)]. First‑line can do the initial SAP spool check and collect basic evidence, but escalate quickly to Infrastructure/Platform for driver, crash‑dump and snapshot analysis. What to tell users now (short) - Tell stakeholders you will not do an immediate production rollback. You will (a) preserve the snapshot(s) and collect crash dumps and event logs, (b) attempt a low‑risk share toggle in a controlled manner after capturing logs if jobs already appear on the Windows queue, and (c) reproduce any rollback on a cloned VM before considering a production rollback. This prevents destroying forensic evidence and repeating BSODs seen previously [Data: Sources (29); Reports (16, 173)]. If you want - I can paste a short PowerShell evidence‑collection script you can run on a printserver (exports Get-PrinterDriver, Get-HotFix, event logs, and minidump copy) and a one‑line hypervisor export request to send to the virtualization team to preserve the snapshot and export VM memory for analysis.

question_text:
Hi IT, we’re having problems with our external file transfer again and I can’t tell if it’s the server or network.

We have a FileZilla Server running on a virtual server (the customer only knows it as 'the FTP box'). External users say they can’t upload anymore; from their side it hangs and then times out. Internally, the server seems reachable and people assume 'it’s up'. On one customer workstation we tried multiple login tests with FTP and SFTP and also WinSCP. We checked local client settings, but it didn’t clearly change anything.

We also asked Network to look at it and they said they did a firewall analysis, and the Core Team checked FileZilla site settings, but it’s still unclear.

What should be the likely diagnosis category and the next checks, and please confirm: 1st-level or 2nd-level ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Summary (short) - Based on the ticket notes, the FileZilla service is running on the VM named KF14azedi01 (10.10.10.10:21). Helpdesk ran client tests (WinSCP: FTP/SFTP) using account Ct_001680; Network performed a firewall analysis and the Core Team checked FileZilla site settings - but the ticket still lacks conclusive server logs / packet captures. The record also shows external FTP access was explicitly decommissioned (firewall policy removal) on 17.12.2024 [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3, 32, 93, 128)]. Likely diagnosis (prioritized) 1) Passive‑FTP data channel blocked / NAT misconfiguration (most likely): control (port 21) connects but the data connection (PASV ports) is not being established because passive ports are blocked or not NAT/forwarded. This is the classic cause when internal reachability is OK but external uploads hang. 2) FileZilla passive-mode or "masquerade/external IP" misconfiguration: server advertising an internal IP or an unforwarded port range in PASV replies. 3) Perimeter policy removal / deliberate decommissioning: ticket shows the external FTP policy was removed on 17.12.24 - if still in effect, external uploads will time out by design. 4) Lower probability: host‑side (Windows) firewall, FileZilla limits, or account/permission issues (would usually show different log symptoms). (These categories are the same prioritized checks recommended in the incident notes and summaries [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3, 32, 93)].) Next checks - ordered, actionable (what to run now) 1) Confirm ownership & service state - Who owns KF14azedi01 and can get server logs / run tcpdump? Ticket shows ownership/installer is unclear; confirm owner before making changes [Data: Sources (34); Reports (128)]. 2) Check whether external FTP is currently allowed - Ask Network to confirm whether the firewall/NAT rule that published FTP (external->10.10.10.10:21) exists or was removed (17.12.24 change is recorded). If it's absent, that explains the timeouts immediately [Data: Sources (34); Reports (93)]. 3) Collect server-side log + config (1st step for Helpdesk / 1st‑level) - Pull FileZilla Server logs covering the failing attempt(s); filter for username Ct_001680 and look for PASV/PORT replies and 425/426/timeout errors. Export FileZilla passive-mode settings (passive port range and "external IP / masquerade" setting) and netstat/ss output showing listening ports. (If you cannot access, escalate to the person who owns the VM.) 4) Reproduce and capture (coordinate with Network & Core) - Coordinate a short, scheduled reproduction window and simultaneously capture: - Perimeter/firewall pcap (ingress/egress) and firewall logs for the test window. - A pcap on the FTP VM (or its hypervisor host) capturing port 21 and the configured passive-range. - Ensure clocks/NTP are synchronized for accurate correlation. 5) Quick functional tests while capturing - From an external test host: connect to public IP:21, issue PASV and note the 227 reply. Compute data port (p1*256+p2). From that client try a TCP connect to that data port to test reachability. From an internal host run equivalent test to compare behavior. Exact artifacts to gather before escalation (minimum packet of evidence) - FileZilla Server log lines (timestamped) with the failing sessions (USER, PASV/PORT, 150/226/425/426 or TLS errors). - Server netstat/ss showing listeners (port 21 and passive-range). - Client-side WinSCP debug log for the failing attempt (with timestamps and client IP). - Perimeter firewall logs (allow/deny) and NAT/conntrack snapshot for the same timestamps. - PCAPs: perimeter interface and server NIC for the same reproduce window. If you need a concise list to hand to the admins, I can paste one you can copy/paste. What to ask Network (copy‑paste example) "Please produce a pcap on the Internet‑facing and LAN‑facing firewall interfaces for YYYY‑MM‑DD HH:MM -> HH:MM UTC, full snaplen (-s 0), and provide firewall logs filtered for dst=10.10.10.10 and dst ports 21 and <passive-port-range>. Also export the NAT/conntrack snapshot for the same window and confirm whether the external FTP publish rule exists or was removed." (If you want, I'll format a ready-to-send message and include the passive-port-range filter once you confirm the server setting.) Ticket level: 1st‑level or 2nd‑level? - Start as 1st‑level (Helpdesk) to gather the artifacts listed above (FileZilla logs, netstat, client WinSCP logs, exact failing timestamps and client IP). The incident notes explicitly advise that 1st‑line should collect logs and capture evidence before escalating [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3)]. - Escalate to 2nd‑level (Network / Core Engineering) when either: - Evidence shows control channel works but data‑channel SYNs never reach the server (network/NAT/firewall problem), or - You lack privileges to collect pcap / firewall NAT state or to change NAT/firewall rules, or - Server logs show binding/passive‑port issues that require server‑owner intervention. The ticket history recommends precisely this flow: 1st‑level collects and 2nd‑level acts on firewall/NAT or privileged server changes [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3)]. Short checklist / next 60‑minute plan 1. Confirm owner and authorization to run captures. 2. Helpdesk: pull FileZilla logs (±15 min around reported failure), netstat, and get the user's WinSCP log and client public IP. 3. Coordinate a 5-10 minute reproduce window with Network and Core. 4. Network: capture perimeter pcap + export NAT/session table for the window. Core: capture server pcap and tail FileZilla logs. 5. Correlate: look for PASV 227 reply IP (internal vs public) and whether client SYNs to the PASV port are translated and forwarded to 10.10.10.10. Brief protocol note (general knowledge) - FTP (PASV) uses a control channel on 21 and separate ephemeral server ports for data; NAT/firewall must allow and/or rewrite the PASV reply and forward the passive port range. If the server advertises a private IP in PASV replies or the passive ports are not forwarded, external uploads will hang while internal clients succeed [Data: General Knowledge (https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc959)]. If you want, I will: - Draft the exact capture commands/log grep lines for your team (tcpdump/tshark, grep/PowerShell) and a ready‑to‑send Network request email, or - Review the artifacts you collect (pcap + FileZilla logs + firewall logs) and point to the exact failure line(s). Key data anchors I used above (for your traceability) - Ticket and troubleshooting notes identifying the host (KF14azedi01 / 10.10.10.10:21), test account Ct_001680, WinSCP/FTP/SFTP tests, Network/Core involvement and recorded firewall policy removal on 17.12.2024 [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3, 32, 93, 128)]. Tell me what level of access you have (server console / firewall admin / only helpdesk) and I'll produce the exact commands and a copy‑paste request for Network/Core.

question_text:
Hi Helpdesk, we onboarded a new employee and they cannot see any printers at all on their Windows laptop. Everyone else in the same office can print normally, but for this user the printer list is completely empty (not just one printer missing — zero). The user is on the correct Wi-Fi, internet works, and they can access SharePoint and everything else.

We already rebooted the laptop and signed out/in once, but it did not change. The user was working from home yesterday and installed a 'PDF printer' tool, but I doubt that matters. Also their mouse sometimes lags when the laptop is on the dock, but again probably unrelated.

What is the most likely cause in this pattern and what exact steps should we do first, and please confirm: should this be a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Summary - quickest diagnosis - Most likely cause: a user‑specific client / identity configuration problem - either the printer‑mapping GPO didn't apply to this user session (so zero automapped printers) or the local Print Spooler / driver state on the laptop is preventing enumeration. The pattern (new user sees zero printers while colleagues on the same Wi‑Fi can print) matches GPO not applied or client‑side spooler/driver problems in the incident corpus [Data: Sources (39); Sources (41)]. - Less likely but important to check next: an explicit Deny ACE on the server queue or a network/VLAN reachability issue between the print server and device (these are typical second‑tier causes when GPOs and client checks are clean) [Data: Sources (3); Sources (22)]. Exact first steps (1st‑level triage - run these in order; copy/pasteable) 1. Restart Print Spooler (fast, safe): - Elevated CMD: net stop spooler && net start spooler - Or PowerShell (Admin): Stop-Service -Name Spooler -Force; Start-Service -Name Spooler - After restart: check Settings -> Printers & scanners. If printers reappear, run step 2 and verify persistence. (Spooler restart is a standard first‑line check) [Data: Sources (41)]. 2. Force Group Policy and re‑login: - In an interactive session (as the user): gpupdate /force - Have the user sign out and sign back in (or reboot). - Check Printers & scanners again. In the dataset forcing GPUpdate restored mappings for new users in multiple cases [Data: Sources (39)]. 3. Collect gpresult and group membership if printers still absent (attach to ticket): - Run (user session): gpresult /h "%temp%\gpresult.html" (attach that HTML) - Also capture: gpresult /r (console summary) and whoami /groups - These show whether the printer‑assignment GPO applied and whether the user token contains the expected AD groups - the gpresult is the single most diagnostic artifact for this symptom [Data: Sources (39); Sources (41)]. 4. Try manual UNC add and quick network checks: - From the user's session try: \\printserver in Explorer and then Add a printer -> "The printer that I want isn't listed" -> Select a shared printer by name -> \\printserver\printer (use the canonical printserver name you have). - Also run: ping printserver and net view \\printserver - Interpretation: if manual UNC add succeeds, GPO mapping was the issue; if UNC add fails with "Access denied" it suggests a queue ACL/deny entry; if it fails with "path not found" or ping fails while colleagues can reach it, suspect network/VLAN [Data: Sources (39); Sources (3); Sources (22)]. 5. If spooler refuses to start or crashes, collect logs and escalate immediately: - Commands to capture (Admin): sc query Spooler ; sc qc Spooler - Export PrintService events: wevtutil epl "Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational" C:\Temp\PrintServiceOperational.evtx - If service crashes or you see spooler-related errors, escalate to 2nd‑level with the EVTX and system/Application logs (spooler crashes may need deeper OS/driver work) [Data: Sources (28); Sources (29)]. What to capture and attach when escalating to 2nd‑level - gpresult.html (from the affected user session) and gpresult /r text. - whoami /groups output. - Screenshot of Settings -> Printers & scanners showing the empty list. - Output of sc query spooler and any PrintService / System Event extracts (EVTX preferred). - If you tried UNC add: exact error text / screenshot from net view \\printserver and ping results. Including these greatly speeds escalation and matches the recommended escalation checklist in the runbooks [Data: Sources (39); Sources (41); Sources (3)]. When to treat this as 1st‑level vs 2nd‑level - Start as a 1st‑level ticket: the basic client checks (spooler restart, gpupdate /force, gpresult, manual UNC add, whoami /groups) are standard first‑line actions and often resolve this exact symptom [Data: Sources (39); Sources (41)]. - Escalate to 2nd‑level/print‑server or AD/Identity when any of the following are true: - gpresult shows the printer‑assignment GPO was applied but printers still do not appear (indicates server‑side ACL or publishing issues) [Data: Sources (3)]; - UNC add fails with "Access denied" (queue explicit Deny) - require print‑server ACL edits by 2nd‑level [Data: Sources (3)]; - network/VLAN reachability to the print server or printers differs from colleagues (ping fails on this laptop but works for others) - escalate to Network [Data: Sources (22)]; - the Print Spooler service crashes or will not start (requires deeper OS/driver or forensic work) [Data: Sources (28)]. Notes on the user's recent PDF‑printer install and dock/mouse lag - A recently installed virtual "PDF printer" can sometimes install drivers or filters that interfere with enumeration; it's worth temporarily uninstalling it if the quick checks above don't help, but this is lower probability than GPO not applied or spooler state [Data: Sources (39); Sources (41)]. - Mouse lag while docked is unlikely related; treat it separately unless you see correlated spooler/driver errors. One‑line message you can paste to the helpdesk technician / user - "Run (as the user): net stop spooler && net start spooler; then gpupdate /force, sign out and sign back in. If printers are still empty, run gpresult /h \"%temp%\\gpresult.html\" and whoami /groups and attach those files/screenshots to the ticket. Also try opening \\printserver and attempting to add \\printserver\\<printer> - capture any error text. Escalate if gpresult shows GPO applied but UNC add returns Access denied or if spooler crashes." If you paste the gpresult.html (or the gpresult /r text) and the net view / ping outputs here I will read them and tell you the exact next remediation or which 2nd‑line team and change to request. References - GPO not applied / gpupdate resolved new‑user missing printers [Data: Sources (39)]. - Explicit Deny ACE on a queue blocked a single user until removed [Data: Sources (3)]. - Print & Scanning standard / decision tree: check GPO, spooler, and queue publishing in order; escalate for queue ACLs, driver/OS or network/VLAN issues [Data: Sources (41); Sources (22)]. If you want, I can also provide a one‑line PowerShell script to collect gpresult, spooler status and the last PrintService events into a ZIP you can attach to the ticket.

question_text:
Hi Helpdesk, one user in our Basel office can see the main office printer, but the moment they hit Print it fails instantly with 'Access denied'. It does not matter if they print from Word, Outlook, or a browser — it is immediate every time. Nothing even seems to reach the printer.

Everyone else sitting in the same room can print without any problem, and the printer itself is working. We rebooted the PC and the printer, tried a different app, and even removed and re-added the printer, but the same 'Access denied' happens right away.

The user recently changed teams internally (new manager / new department), so there may have been group changes, but they should still be allowed to print like everyone else. What is the most likely root cause and what should we check first, and please confirm: should this be a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Summary - short answer - Most likely root cause: an explicit Deny ACE on the MFP‑BASEL‑01 print queue (a per‑user deny overrides the group Allow and produces the immediate "Access denied" behaviour you describe). Secondary possibility (if queue ACLs look OK) is server->printer reachability blocked by VLAN/firewall rules. [Data: Sources (3); Sources (22); Reports (169)]. - Start this as a 1st‑level ticket for the quick checks below. Escalate to 2nd‑level (print‑server / AD / Network teams) if you find a Deny that needs removal, AD membership changes, or a server->printer connectivity/VLAN block. [Data: Sources (3); Sources (41)]. Why I say that (evidence from Basel incidents) - Multiple Basel incident records show the exact symptom (user sees the printer, every app fails instantly with "Access denied", jobs never reach the device) was caused by an explicit Deny ACE on the queue; removing the Deny restored printing. Deny ACEs take precedence over AD group Allow entries. [Data: Sources (3); Reports (169, 94)]. - A different Basel incident shows a printer moved into a PRINTER VLAN where the print server could not reach it until the Network Team adjusted a firewall rule - jobs stayed in queue or disappeared in that case. That's why reachability is the secondary check. [Data: Sources (22); Sources (41)]. Immediate checks to run now (1st‑level, do these first - fast) 1. Capture the user identity and a failure timestamp. Ask the user to run whoami (or tell you their UPN) and perform one timed test print so you have an exact timestamp to correlate logs. 2. Confirm AD group membership (quick): check the user is still in the location print group (PRT‑Basel or equivalent). If you can't check AD directly, note to escalate. [Data: Sources (3); Sources (39)] - Quick commands (helpdesk): whoami /upn or on an admin host: Get-ADUser -Identity <samAccountName> -Properties MemberOf. 3. Force policy and re‑test (often fixes GPO timing issues): on the user PC run (as the user) gpupdate /force, then have them sign out and sign back in and retry printing. If this fixes it, the problem was GPO timing; if not, continue. [Data: Sources (39); Sources (2)] 4. Verify whether the print job appears on the print server: - While the user does a failing print, have a print‑server admin watch the queue (Print Management) or check PrintService/Operational for events at that timestamp. If the job never reaches the server and you still see "Access denied", that points strongly to queue ACL rejection. [Data: Sources (3)] 5. Quick cross‑test: have the user sign into a colleague's PC (same room) and print, or have a colleague sign into the user's PC and print. This isolates user token vs PC vs server/ACL cause. [Data: Sources (3)] If you find an immediate Deny on the queue (what it means and next step) - If the queue Security tab shows an explicit Deny ACE for the user or their SID, that is the proximate cause - remove the Deny (with change control / approval), then have the user log off/on and test. Removing the Deny resolved the Basel incident. This requires print‑server/admin rights (2nd‑level). [Data: Sources (3); Reports (169)]. If the queue ACL looks clean (what to check next) 1. Server->printer connectivity: from the print server run ping <printer_IP>, Test‑NetConnection -ComputerName <printer_IP> -Port 9100 (or the port in use). If the print server cannot reach the printer while local clients can, escalate to Network - Basel had this exact VLAN/firewall pattern. [Data: Sources (22); Sources (41)] 2. Inspect PrintService (Operational) and System event logs on the print server for errors at the failing timestamp - export the filtered EVTX or CSV for the 2nd‑line team if needed. (If you want, I can paste the PowerShell commands for these exports.) [Data: Sources (3)] Who should handle what - 1st vs 2nd level - 1st‑level (Helpdesk): collect username + timestamps, confirm AD group membership (or capture whoami), run gpupdate /force, reproduce and capture screenshots, attempt colleague cross‑test, and confirm whether job reaches the server. These are fast triage steps and often resolve mapping/GPO problems. [Data: Sources (39); Sources (2)] - 2nd‑level (Print‑server / AD / Network team): required if you must edit queue ACLs (remove explicit Deny), fix AD group nesting or membership on the directory, or change firewall/routing rules between print server and printer VLAN. Queue ACL edits and network firewall changes are server/infra work and should be handled by 2nd‑level. [Data: Sources (3); Sources (22); Sources (41)] Copy‑paste escalation text for 2nd‑level (use in ticket) Title: Escalation - Immediate "Access denied" for user printing to MFP‑BASEL‑01 (user: <UPN/sAMAccountName>) Body (paste): - Symptom: User can see MFP‑BASEL‑01 but every print attempt (Word/Outlook/Browser) fails instantly with "Access denied". Jobs do not appear on the server queue. Colleagues in the same room can print OK. Reboots, remove/re-add printer, gpupdate done (still fails). Occurred after internal team move. - Actions done (by Helpdesk): captured timestamp <YYYY‑MM‑DD HH:MM TZ>, whoami/UPN: <user>, gpupdate /force run, test print reproduced at <timestamp>, colleague cross‑test performed. - Request: please inspect MFP‑BASEL‑01 queue ACL (Print Management -> Properties -> Security) and check for any explicit Deny ACE for the affected user or any SID matching their account; if present, please remove/rectify per change control and test with the user. If no Deny present, please export PrintService Operational logs for ±15 minutes around <timestamp> and confirm server->printer reachability (ping / TCP 9100). If ping fails, provide firewall rule/deny logs for the print‑server subnet -> printer VLAN. - Attachments: gpresult (if available), screenshot of client error, timestamp of failing attempt. (Reference: prior Basel incidents where explicit Deny on queue or printer VLAN firewall caused identical behaviour.) [Data: Sources (3); Sources (22); Sources (41)]. A couple of operational tips - Don't add an individual Allow for the user as a long‑term fix - remove the Deny or fix group membership and follow the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard (group‑based ACLs only). [Data: Sources (41); Reports (169)] - Capture screenshots of the Security tab before/after any change and record who authorized the edit (audit trail). [Data: Reports (169)] If you want, I can: - Provide the exact PowerShell commands to export PrintService logs, enumerate printer ACLs, or dump Get‑Printer/ Get‑PrinterPort output for your print admin to run; or - Draft the ticket body prefilled with your user's UPN and the failing‑print timestamps for immediate submission. References (selected) - Explicit Deny on MFP‑BASEL‑01 queue caused immediate "Access denied"; removal restored printing [Data: Sources (3); Reports (169)]. - Printer moved into PRINTER VLAN and print server could not reach it until firewall rule adjusted (server->printer reachability issue) [Data: Sources (22); Sources (41)]. - GPO/printer mapping and gpupdate /force used during triage in related tickets [Data: Sources (39); Sources (2)]. If you paste the user's UPN and a single reproducible timestamp here I'll give the exact command lines and a ready‑to‑send escalation note prefilled with those values.
